query: secure CORS handling frontend interview question

selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No exact-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this browser, network, security, or state-management prompt, so broader JavaScript question-bank pages would be weaker competitors than topic-direct sources.

competitors.label: MDN CORS
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference for cross-origin requests, preflight behavior, credential rules, and browser-enforced CORS boundaries.
theirStrengths: It is authoritative on CORS semantics, preflights, headers, and credential restrictions.
theirGaps: It is less interview-shaped on the architectural answer that frontend code cannot bypass CORS and should move to a proxy or BFF when necessary.

realWorldUseCases: Secure CORS handling is mostly a server-policy and preflight debugging problem, not a frontend workaround. The production-safe fixes are trusted origin allowlists, correct headers, and a BFF or proxy when direct browser access is the wrong architecture.
actionableExamples: Preflight (OPTIONS) requests Browsers send a preflight when a request is not “simple” (custom headers, non-GET/POST, or JSON content type). Your server must respond to OPTIONS with the same CORS headers or the real request will be blocked.
followUpConfusion: Avoid these - Disabling CORS in the browser or using extensions (not a real fix). - Using mode: 'no-cors' (opaque responses you cannot read). - Setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with credentials.

realWorldUseCases: MDN defines CORS correctly, but our page is more explicit about the operational answer interviewers want: server policy, preflight debug, and a BFF when browser access is the wrong design.
actionableExamples: The canonical docs still have strong header and preflight examples, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is clearer on the likely follow-up traps: no-cors is not a fix, wildcard origins break with credentials, and browser extensions are not production answers.

actionableExamples: Add one shorter credentialed-request example later if we need more example distance over MDN.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical CORS reference, but our page is stronger for this interview question because it translates the spec into server-policy decisions and safe frontend architecture.

competitors.label: web.dev Same-origin fetch codelab
competitors.selectionReason: web.dev provides practical browser-network guidance and is a strong tutorial comparator for same-origin and cross-origin request behavior.
theirStrengths: It is practical on browser request behavior and origin boundaries.
theirGaps: It is less explicit on the secure production answer for third-party APIs: use a BFF or proxy rather than hunting for a frontend-only workaround.

realWorldUseCases: Secure CORS handling is mostly a server-policy and preflight debugging problem, not a frontend workaround. The production-safe fixes are trusted origin allowlists, correct headers, and a BFF or proxy when direct browser access is the wrong architecture.
actionableExamples: Preflight (OPTIONS) requests Browsers send a preflight when a request is not “simple” (custom headers, non-GET/POST, or JSON content type). Your server must respond to OPTIONS with the same CORS headers or the real request will be blocked.
followUpConfusion: Avoid these - Disabling CORS in the browser or using extensions (not a real fix). - Using mode: 'no-cors' (opaque responses you cannot read). - Setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with credentials.

realWorldUseCases: web.dev is practical on browser behavior, but our page is more explicit about the production boundary between frontend code and server-enforced origin policy.
actionableExamples: Its guidance is still practical and example-driven, so the example criterion remains tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is stronger on the next architectural questions: proxy/BFF fallback, credentials, and why frontend workarounds are the wrong mental model.

actionableExamples: Keep the avoid-these list close to the preflight section so the answer stays more operational than a browser-network tutorial.

competitors.winReason: web.dev is practical on origin rules, but our page is stronger because it frames CORS as a server-policy and architecture problem instead of a frontend trick question.

competitors.label: Stack Overflow no Access-Control-Allow-Origin header
competitors.selectionReason: This is a widely referenced community Q&A for the classic CORS error and preflight confusion frontend developers actually hit.
theirStrengths: It is tightly focused on the exact runtime error most developers see first.
theirGaps: It is narrower than the interview prompt and less explicit on trusted-origin policy, proxy architecture, and credential safety.

realWorldUseCases: Secure CORS handling is mostly a server-policy and preflight debugging problem, not a frontend workaround. The production-safe fixes are trusted origin allowlists, correct headers, and a BFF or proxy when direct browser access is the wrong architecture.
actionableExamples: Preflight (OPTIONS) requests Browsers send a preflight when a request is not “simple” (custom headers, non-GET/POST, or JSON content type). Your server must respond to OPTIONS with the same CORS headers or the real request will be blocked.
followUpConfusion: Avoid these - Disabling CORS in the browser or using extensions (not a real fix). - Using mode: 'no-cors' (opaque responses you cannot read). - Setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with credentials.

realWorldUseCases: The thread is useful for one error message, but our page gives the fuller production answer: fix server policy or move the call behind a backend you control.
